The code uses a generic catch-all exception handler (`with _ ->`), which suppresses all exceptions without logging them. This could make debugging difficult if date parsing fails. Consider logging the exception or at least the exception message to help diagnose issues when they occur.
```suggestion
                            with ex ->
                                printfn $"[WARN] Failed to parse mergedAt date: '%s{mergedAtStr}'. Exception: %s{ex.Message}"
```

The query includes `-author:app/robot` but the filtering logic checks for usernames ending with `[bot]` (line 520). This creates a potential inconsistency where some bots might be excluded at the query level while others are excluded at the filtering level. Consider either removing `-author:app/robot` from the query or documenting why both filters are necessary.
```suggestion
                    $"pr list -S \"state:closed base:main closed:>{date}\" --json commits,mergedAt"
```

Similarly, there's no validation that `jsonOutput` is not empty before parsing. While the code checks if `queryResult.ExitCode <> 0`, it doesn't check if `StandardOutput` is empty or contains valid JSON. Consider adding validation to ensure the output is not empty before calling `FSharp.Data.JsonValue.Parse`.
```suggestion
            if String.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(jsonOutput) then
                printfn "Warning: No PRs found or empty JSON output from gh CLI."
                String.Empty
            else
                // Parse JSON to filter by mergedAt timestamp
                let jsonValue = FSharp.Data.JsonValue.Parse(jsonOutput)
                let prs = jsonValue.AsArray()

                // Parse the date as ISO 8601 format (GitHub always returns dates in this format: "2025-08-02T10:25:30Z")
                let cutoffTimestamp =
                    DateTime.Parse(date, null, System.Globalization.DateTimeStyles.RoundtripKind).ToUniversalTime()

                printfn $"Filtering PRs merged after: {cutoffTimestamp:O}"

                let authors =
                    prs
                    |> Array.collect (fun (pr: FSharp.Data.JsonValue) ->
                        let mergedAtOpt =
                            match pr.TryGetProperty("mergedAt") with
                            | Some mergedAtJson ->
                                try
                                    let mergedAtStr = mergedAtJson.AsString()
                                    Some(
                                        DateTime
                                            .Parse(mergedAtStr, null, System.Globalization.DateTimeStyles.RoundtripKind)
                                            .ToUniversalTime()
                                    )
                                with _ ->
                                    None
                            | None -> None

                        match mergedAtOpt with
                        | Some mergedAt when mergedAt > cutoffTimestamp ->
                            match pr.TryGetProperty("commits") with
                            | Some commitsJson ->
                                let commits = commitsJson.AsArray()
                                commits
                                |> Array.collect (fun (commit: FSharp.Data.JsonValue) ->
                                    match commit.TryGetProperty("authors") with
                                    | Some authorsJson ->
                                        let commitAuthors = authorsJson.AsArray()
                                        commitAuthors
                                        |> Array.choose (fun (author: FSharp.Data.JsonValue) ->
                                            match author.TryGetProperty("login") with
                                            | Some loginJson ->
                                                let login = loginJson.AsString()
                                                // Filter out bots
                                                if login.EndsWith("[bot]", StringComparison.Ordinal) then
                                                    None
                                                else
                                                    Some(login)
                                            | None -> None)
                                    | None -> [||])
                            | None -> [||]
                        | _ -> [||])
                    |> Array.distinct
                    |> Array.sort

                printfn $"Found {authors.Length} contributors for this release"

                if authors.Length = 0 then
                    String.Empty
                elif authors.Length = 1 then
                    $"Special thanks to @%s{authors.[0]}!"
                else
                    let lastAuthor = Array.last authors
                    let otherAuthors =
                        if authors.Length = 2 then
                            $"@{authors.[0]}"
                        else
                            authors
                            |> Array.take (authors.Length - 1)
                            |> Array.map (sprintf "@%s")
                            |> String.concat ", "
                    $"Special thanks to %s{otherAuthors} and @%s{lastAuthor}!"
```

The example date `01/2025` is formatted differently than the example on line 88 (`11/2022`). While both are valid month/year formats, for consistency, consider using the same format (either `MM/YYYY` or `M/YYYY`) throughout the documentation.
```suggestion
- `# 7.0.0 <sub>Year Zero</sub> - 1/2025`
```

This should probably be updated to `2025-12-12` to match the actual prerelease version in the CHANGELOG, or use a more generic future date like `2026-01-15` to make it clear this is an example.

```suggestion
## [8.0.0-alpha-001] - 2026-01-15
```

The changelog filtering logic has been removed (previously filtered out prereleases with line 567), which means the code now takes the last 2 releases regardless of whether they are prereleases. This could cause issues if there are multiple consecutive prerelease versions in the changelog, as the "last release" used for author attribution might not be the intended stable release. Consider whether this behavior is intentional or if additional filtering logic is needed.

The date parsing logic tries three different formats sequentially with nested try-catch blocks. The third format (yyyy-MM-dd) would be reached if both previous parses fail, but it could still throw an exception that would propagate up. Consider adding a final catch block or using TryParseExact to handle the case where all parsing attempts fail gracefully.

The fallback logic here duplicates the date calculation multiple times (lines 442, 446, 450, 454). Consider extracting the fallback date creation to a single location before the try-catch block to improve maintainability and reduce code duplication.

Multiple catch-all exception handlers (lines 501-502, 510-524, 525-526) silently swallow exceptions and return empty arrays. This makes debugging difficult when something goes wrong with PR commit parsing. Consider adding logging with the exception details to understand what's failing, as this directly impacts the accuracy of contributor attribution in release notes.

The error handling here uses a catch-all exception handler with no logging or specific error message. When the JSON parsing fails, it silently falls back to the current date without indicating what went wrong. Consider logging the exception message to help diagnose parsing failures, especially since this affects contributor attribution.
```suggestion
                    printfn $"Could not parse GitHub release JSON: %s, using current date: {fallbackDate}" ex.Message
```

The example date "2024-12-12" is used here, but it's inconsistent with line 89 which shows "01/2025" for version 7.0.0. Consider using a consistent date format in examples throughout the documentation.
```suggestion
## [8.0.0-alpha-001] - 12/2024
```

The workflow name "Build main" is misleading since this workflow's purpose is to release packages to NuGet, not just to build. Consider renaming to "Release" or "Publish to NuGet" to better reflect its purpose.
```suggestion
name: Release
```

The workflow is missing explicit `permissions` configuration. For a release workflow that publishes packages, it's a security best practice to explicitly declare the minimum required permissions. At minimum, this workflow needs:
```yaml
permissions:
  contents: write  # for creating GitHub releases
  id-token: write  # for OIDC authentication with NuGet (if using trusted publishing)
```

This follows the principle of least privilege and makes the workflow's security requirements explicit.

The implementation doesn't appear to use NuGet's trusted publishing (OIDC-based authentication). True trusted publishing would eliminate the need for API keys and the `NuGet/login` action. Instead, this still relies on the `NUGET_API_KEY` output from the login action, which defeats the purpose of trusted publishing. 

For actual trusted publishing, you should:
1. Configure trusted publishing in your NuGet.org account settings for this package
2. Remove the `NuGet/login` action step
3. Modify the `dotnet nuget push` command in `pushPackage` function to use the `--skip-duplicate` flag without requiring an API key when running in GitHub Actions with proper permissions

Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/nuget/nuget-org/publish-with-github-actions

```suggestion
    - name: "Publish"
      env:
```

The `--no-restore` flag in the Pack stage assumes that a restore has already been performed. However, the Build stage also doesn't include a restore step. This could cause the pack operation to fail if dependencies aren't already restored. Consider either removing the `--no-restore` flag or adding an explicit restore step before the Build stage.
```suggestion
    workingDir __SOURCE_DIRECTORY__
    stage "Restore" { run "dotnet restore" }
```

Same issue as in oak.fsx: The logic for determining `isSignature` is incorrect. The script reads the content of the input file into the `sample` variable, then checks if `sample.EndsWith(".fsi")`. This checks if the file *content* ends with ".fsi", not the file *name*. It should be checking the file path instead: `fsi.CommandLineArgs.[fsi.CommandLineArgs.Length - 1].EndsWith(".fsi")`
```suggestion
        let isSignature = fsi.CommandLineArgs.[fsi.CommandLineArgs.Length - 1].EndsWith(".fsi")
```

[Long story](https://amplifyingfsharp.io/sessions/2024-12-13/) :-). 
For the "warn scopes" functionality to make sense, `#nowarn` and `#warnon` must be allowed on any line (also like in the middle of an expression).
Therefore, we can no longer parse them in the parser. We parse them rather during lexing and store them first temporarily in a lexbuf BufferLocalStore (similar to #ifdef and xml comments) and later in DiagnosticOptions, which is available where the diagnostic filtering takes place. So, all outside of compilation proper (parsing, syntax trees (except for the trivia), type checking, code gen etc).

Oh, the `.fsproj` story is also interesting. When editing the file manually then IntelliJ rules are used, but when Rider modifies it from code it actually delegates a lot of the work to MSBuild and it doesn't seem to follow what the rest of the IDE does. I'm not sure how easy/feasible it's to intercept the MSBuild modifications, but it seems it was difficult enough to become less prioritized.

I think that a lot of software evolve like that :) A "good enough" solution is often the best starting point, and then when/if it breaks it is easier to design the "good" solution (more knowledge available).

But Rider sure does have some fun formatting quirks :) It is pretty confusing to figure out what settings it applies and when. E.g. when I edit a `.fsproj` file it sort of uses a mix of the "code style" settings for XML-files (specified in the Settings UI), and the settings I have specified in `.editorconfig` – but this is not shown anywhere in settings UI (i.e. which settings are overriden by `.editorconfig` – or the way around). And then when Rider modifies `.fsproj` it uses a whole different set of formatting rules :)

Just a fun story to share: at some point Rider took the actual defaults from Fantomas and hardcoded them to its settings. Later Fantomas has changed the defaults, so the ones in Rider have become outdated, and for some time we were unfortunately stuck with them. It was never an intention to not respect the settings, just a coincidence of how the things have evolved here 🙂

To make it tail recursive you can use the following:

```fsharp
let (|Sequentials|_|) e =
    let rec visit (e: SynExpr) (finalContinuation: SynExpr list -> SynExpr list) : SynExpr list =
        match e with
        | Sequential (e1, e2, _) -> visit e2 (fun xs -> e1 :: xs |> finalContinuation)
        | e -> finalContinuation [ e ]

    match e with
    | Sequential (e1, e2, _) ->
        let xs = visit e2 id
        Some(e1 :: xs)
    | _ -> None
```

The `sepNlnWhenWriteBeforeNewlineNotEmpty` will not really play out when there is a comment after the access modifier.

Something like:
```fsharp
type Person = private // foo
    {
        FirstName: string
        LastName: string
    }
```

would be formatted to

```fsharp
type Person = private // foo
{
    FirstName: string
    LastName: string
}
```

It is better to use `sepSpace` in this case and let the comment flow after the `{` like

```fsharp
type Person = private { // foo
    FirstName: string
    LastName: string
}
```

```fsharp
        genTriviaFor SynExpr_ArrayOrList_OpeningDelimiter openingTokenRange sepOpenLFixed
        +> indent
        +> sepNlnUnlessLastEventIsNewline
        +> (fun (ctx: Context) ->
            let ctx2 = (col sepNln xs (genExpr astContext)) ctx
            let newEvs = ctx2.WriterEvents |> Seq.take (ctx2.WriterEvents.Length - ctx.WriterEvents.Length)
            match Seq.tryHead newEvs with
            | Some WriterEvent.WriteLineBecauseOfTrivia ->
                // apply new events on ctx, except the last one
                let resetModel = 
                    (ctx.WriterModel, Seq.rev(Seq.tail newEvs))
                    ||> Seq.fold (fun m e -> WriterModel.update ctx.Config.MaxLineLength e m)
                let resetContext =
                    { ctx2 with
                        WriterEvents = ctx2.WriterEvents.Tail
                        WriterModel = resetModel }
                (unindent +> sepNlnForTrivia) resetContext
            | _ -> unindent ctx2)
        +> sepNlnUnlessLastEventIsNewline
        +> genTriviaFor SynExpr_ArrayOrList_ClosingDelimiter closingTokenRange sepCloseLFixed
```

I wouldn't pass in the `lids` of the `DotGet` here to the `genAppWithLambda` function.
What you want to control from outside the `genAppWithLambda` is whether there should be a space or not.

I would pass in a function (that adds the space) instead of a boolean.

Something long the lines of:

```
        | DotGetAppWithLambda ((e, es, lpr, lambda, rpr, pr), lids) ->
            leadingExpressionIsMultiline 
                (genAppWithLambda astContext sepNone (e, es, lpr, lambda, rpr, pr))
                (fun isMultline -> // print lids and do the right thing based on isMultline)

        // functionName arg1 arg2 (fun x y z -> ...)
        | AppWithLambda (e, es, lpr, lambda, rpr, pr) ->
              let sepSpaceAfterFunctionName =
                  let sepSpaceBasedOnSetting e =
                      match e with
                      | Paren _ -> sepSpace
                      | UppercaseSynExpr -> (fun ctx -> onlyIf ctx.Config.SpaceBeforeUppercaseInvocation sepSpace ctx)
                      | LowercaseSynExpr -> (fun ctx -> onlyIf ctx.Config.SpaceBeforeLowercaseInvocation sepSpace ctx)

                  match es with
                  | [] -> sepSpaceBasedOnSetting e
                  | _ -> sepSpace

            genAppWithLambda astContext sepSpaceAfterFunctionName  (e, es, lpr, lambda, rpr, pr) 
```

The benefit here is that `genAppWithLambda` has no knowledge about `lids` so the function only does one thing (namely printing a function application that ends with a lambda).

```fsharp
    let genEqualsInBinding (ctx: Context) =
        let equalsRange =

                | Some tn when (List.isNotEmpty tn.ContentAfter) -> sepNone

            let hasGenerics =

            ifElse hasGenerics sepColonWithSpacesFixed sepColon
```

This proves to be more challenging than I anticipated.
In essence, you want to use the start position of the entire top-level let binding (a range that includes the `let` keyword) and the start position of the function name `map`.
You know the `inline` keyword is somewhere in that range.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2621499/149285599-51cf8824-b40d-4a4d-a39f-dbdd551c28b8.png)

And then you end up with something like:
```fsharp
    let afterLetKeyword =
        let genInline (ctx: Context) =
            let letBindingRange : Range = failwith "information not present at this point"
            let estimateRange = ctx.MkRange letBindingRange.Start patRange.Start
            tokN estimateRange INLINE (!- "inline ")

        ifElse isMutable (!- "mutable ") sepNone
        +> ifElse isInline genInline sepNone
        +> opt sepSpace ao genAccess
```

The main problem is that inside that function you don't have any notion of the start of the prefix.
You could be inside of a member and then instead of `let` you started with `member`.
So, you would need to pass down the start position of the parent node of SynBinding.

As there are multiple places where `genSynBindingFunction` is eventually called this would be quite tricky.

An easier route would be if the range of the inline keyword was added to the Syntax tree in the compiler.
Because now we really have to hack our way around it to find an approximate range.
This sounds like a good candidate for SyntaxTrivia, https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/issues/12418.

It turns out that `Gen.nonEmptyListOf` is a thing, by the way.
In fact it turns out that `Gen.choice(0, len - 1)` is a thing, so you can just directly construct what you need.

The reason it's better to construct rather than filter is because:

* it's easier to reason about the distribution of the resulting test cases
* you make FsCheck do less wasted computation in creating test cases only to discard them immediately.

In this case, neither of those is a big deal, but if your condition is hard to satisfy or you're specifically testing some edge case where you want precise control over the distribution, it's better to be in the habit of constructing cases which automatically have the right property.

You're not actually testing a property here, because there are no variables for FsCheck to generalise over. These should just be ordinary test cases, with `[<Test false>]` and `[<Test true>]`. Alternatively you could do `Check.QuickThrowOnFailure property` without supplying `true` and `false`.
Similarly here, you can avoid a lot of filtering with `|> Gen.map abs` and getting rid of the `n >= 0` filter.

Tiny point, you don't have to change anything but I do think it's useful for learning the right mental model: it's generally better to *construct* the right cases rather than to *filter down* to them. Here, you could generate `x : int` and `xs : int list`, and then return `x :: xs`, constructing a nonempty list.

A number of ways this property-based test could be improved, by the way. The most obvious to me: split into three tests, one for `n > List.length xs`, one for `n < List.length xs`, and one for `n = List.length xs`. For example:

```fsharp
open FsCheck
[<Test>]
let ``When n equals list length`` () =
    let property (xs : int list) : bool =
        let actual = List.splitAround (List.length xs) xs
        match actual with
        | None -> false
        | Some (init, None) -> init = xs
        | Some (init, Some _) -> false
    Check.QuickThrowOnFailure property

[<Test>]
let ``When n less than list length`` () =
    let property (xs : int list, n : int) : bool =
        let actual = List.splitAround (List.length xs) xs
        match actual with
        | None -> false
        | Some (init, None) -> init = xs
        | Some (init, Some _) -> false

    let gen =
        gen {
            let! xs = Gen.list<int> // or however you do this
            let len = List.length xs
            let! n = Gen.int |> Gen.filter (fun n -> n < len)
            return (xs, n)
        }

    property
    |> Prop.forAll (Arb.fromGen gen)
    |> Check.QuickThrowOnFailure
```

```fsharp
[<Test>]
let ``my test`` () =
    let property (xs : int list) (n : int) : bool =
        let n = abs n
        let actual = List.splitAround n xs
        match actual with
        | None -> n > List.length xs
        | Some (init, r) ->
            n <= List.length xs &&
            match r with
            | None -> xs = init
            | Some (x, tail) ->
                xs = init @ (x :: tail)
    Check.QuickThrowOnFailure property
```

There's actually a slightly different phrasing of this that I prefer, but I'm still fighting to get the blog post through review :P I don't think there's anything *wrong* with this version, it just has a very odd order of evaluation.

For reference (though I don't think you need to change what you have), my preferred version is:

```fsharp
let rec sequence
    (results : (('result -> 'ret) -> 'ret) list)
    (andThen : 'result list -> 'ret)
    : 'ret
    =
    match results with
    | [] -> andThen []
    | andThenInner :: andThenInners ->
        fun (results : 'result list) ->
            fun (result : 'result) ->
                result :: results
                |> andThen
            |> andThenInner
        |> sequence andThenInner
```
